content.text: A Managed Identity is a special type of service principal that Azure automatically creates and manages for a resource. The Azure platform provisions the cryptographic credentials, rotates them continuously, and injects them into the resource's runtime environment via the Instance Metadata Service (IMDS). Your code acquires tokens by calling a local HTTP endpoint — no credentials to store, rotate, or leak.

attrs.alt: Managed Identity token flow via IMDS: app calls local endpoint, platform attests identity, Entra ID issues JWT

content.text: Why Managed Identities

The fundamental problem with service accounts and client secrets is that every secret is a liability: it can be logged, checked into source control, stolen from a CI/CD variable, or left to expire at the worst possible time. Managed Identities eliminate all of these risks:
No credential to store — the Azure platform holds the private key; your code never sees it
No rotation required — the platform rotates credentials continuously and transparently
No accidental exposure — there is nothing to check into source control or leak in a log
Automatic cleanup — system-assigned MIs are deleted when the resource is deleted
Free — no additional cost beyond the resources that use them

If your workload runs on Azure, use a Managed Identity. Reserve service principals with secrets or certificates for workloads running outside Azure (GitHub Actions with OIDC is the exception — use federated credentials there).
System-Assigned vs User-Assigned
Aspect
System-Assigned
User-Assigned
Creation
Enabled directly on a resource
Created as a standalone ARM resource
Lifecycle
Tied to the parent resource — deleted when resource is deleted
Independent — survives resource deletion
Cardinality
One MI per resource
One MI can be assigned to many resources
Use case
Single-purpose resource with unique identity
Shared identity across a fleet (VMs, containers, functions)
Principal ID
One per resource
One per MI resource; same across all assigned resources
Role assignment target
Service principal of the specific resource
Service principal of the MI (reused across resources)
Acquiring Tokens: The IMDS Pattern
Using the Azure SDK (recommended)

from azure.identity import ManagedIdentityCredential
from azure.keyvault.secrets import SecretClient
from azure.storage.blob import BlobServiceClient
from azure.mgmt.resource import ResourceManagementClient

# ManagedIdentityCredential detects the IMDS endpoint automatically
# Works on: VMs, App Service, Functions, AKS (pod identity / workload identity),
#           Container Apps, Container Instances, Service Fabric
credential = ManagedIdentityCredential()

# Key Vault
kv = SecretClient("https://myvault.vault.azure.net", credential)
print(kv.get_secret("db-password").value)

# Blob Storage
blob = BlobServiceClient("https://mystg.blob.core.windows.net", credential)
for container in blob.list_containers():
    print(container["name"])

# Azure Management API
rmc = ResourceManagementClient(credential, "<subscription-id>")
for rg in rmc.resource_groups.list():
    print(rg.name)
Raw HTTP Call to IMDS (any language)

import requests

# Raw HTTP call to IMDS — works in any language without an SDK
IMDS_ENDPOINT = "http://169.254.169.254/metadata/identity/oauth2/token"

resp = requests.get(
    IMDS_ENDPOINT,
    headers={"Metadata": "true"},
    params={
        "api-version": "2018-02-01",
        "resource": "https://management.azure.com/"
        # For storage: "https://storage.azure.com/"
        # For Key Vault: "https://vault.azure.net"
        # For Graph:     "https://graph.microsoft.com/"
    }
)
token_data = resp.json()
access_token = token_data["access_token"]
expires_on   = token_data["expires_on"]   # Unix timestamp
print(f"Token acquired, expires: {expires_on}")

# Cache the token until expires_on - 60 seconds
# The Azure SDKs handle this automatically

The IMDS endpoint (169.254.169.254) is a link-local address only reachable from within the Azure resource. It is not accessible from outside the VM, container, or function — making it an inherently secure token acquisition mechanism.
Enabling System-Assigned Managed Identity

# Enable system-assigned MI on a VM
az vm identity assign     --name "vm-appserver"     --resource-group "rg-production"

# Output: principalId (the MI's service principal object ID)
# {
#   "systemAssignedIdentity": "xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx",
#   "userAssignedIdentities": {}
# }

# Get the principal ID programmatically
MI_PRINCIPAL=$(az vm show     --name vm-appserver     --resource-group rg-production     --query "identity.principalId" -o tsv)

# Grant the MI Reader on a resource group
az role assignment create     --assignee "$MI_PRINCIPAL"     --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal     --role "Key Vault Secrets User"     --scope "/subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-production/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/myvault"

from azure.mgmt.compute import ComputeManagementClient
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
compute_client = ComputeManagementClient(credential, "<subscription-id>")

# Enable system-assigned MI via SDK
vm = compute_client.virtual_machines.get("rg-production", "vm-appserver")
vm.identity = {"type": "SystemAssigned"}
poller = compute_client.virtual_machines.begin_create_or_update(
    "rg-production", "vm-appserver", vm
)
updated_vm = poller.result()
print("MI Principal ID:", updated_vm.identity.principal_id)
Creating and Assigning User-Assigned Managed Identity

# Create a user-assigned MI (standalone resource)
az identity create     --name "mi-myapp"     --resource-group "rg-identity"     --location "eastus"

# Output: principalId, clientId (use clientId to target it in code)
MI_CLIENT_ID=$(az identity show     --name mi-myapp     --resource-group rg-identity     --query clientId -o tsv)

MI_RESOURCE_ID=$(az identity show     --name mi-myapp     --resource-group rg-identity     --query id -o tsv)

# Assign the MI to multiple VMs
az vm identity assign     --name "vm-web-1"     --resource-group "rg-production"     --identities "$MI_RESOURCE_ID"

az vm identity assign     --name "vm-web-2"     --resource-group "rg-production"     --identities "$MI_RESOURCE_ID"

# In code: target the user-assigned MI by clientId
from azure.identity import ManagedIdentityCredential
credential = ManagedIdentityCredential(client_id=MI_CLIENT_ID)

from azure.mgmt.msi import ManagedServiceIdentityClient
from azure.mgmt.authorization import AuthorizationManagementClient
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
import uuid

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
msi_client = ManagedServiceIdentityClient(credential, "<subscription-id>")

# Create user-assigned MI
mi = msi_client.user_assigned_identities.create_or_update(
    resource_group_name="rg-identity",
    resource_name="mi-myapp",
    parameters={"location": "eastus"}
)
print("MI client ID:   ", mi.client_id)
print("MI principal ID:", mi.principal_id)

# Assign a role to the MI
auth_client = AuthorizationManagementClient(credential, "<subscription-id>")
SECRETS_USER_ROLE = "/subscriptions/<sub-id>/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/roleDefinitions/4633458b-17de-408a-b874-0445c86b69e6"

auth_client.role_assignments.create(
    scope="/subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-production/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/myvault",
    role_assignment_name=str(uuid.uuid4()),
    parameters={"properties": {"role_definition_id": SECRETS_USER_ROLE, "principal_id": mi.principal_id, "principal_type": "ServicePrincipal"}}
)
Managed Identity on Different Azure Services
Service
Enable System-Assigned MI
Notes
Virtual Machine
az vm identity assign --name <vm> --resource-group <rg>
Supports both system and user-assigned
App Service / Functions
az webapp identity assign --name <app> --resource-group <rg>
Available in all tiers including Consumption (Functions)
AKS
Enable with --enable-managed-identity; use workload identity for pod-level
Cluster-level MI for control plane; pod-level via workload identity federation
Container Apps
Set in container app environment configuration
Supports both; recommended for all container apps
Logic Apps
Identity tab in the Azure portal / ARM template
Standard tier only for system-assigned
Azure Data Factory
Automatically created when ADF instance is created
Used for linked service authentication
Azure Automation
Enable from Automation Account > Identity
Used for runbooks to access other Azure resources
App Service Example

# Enable system-assigned MI on an App Service
az webapp identity assign     --name "app-mywebapp"     --resource-group "rg-production"

# Get the principal ID
MI_PRINCIPAL=$(az webapp show     --name app-mywebapp     --resource-group rg-production     --query "identity.principalId" -o tsv)

# Grant Key Vault Secrets User
az role assignment create     --assignee "$MI_PRINCIPAL"     --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal     --role "Key Vault Secrets User"     --scope "/subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-production/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/myvault"

# In application code (Python):
# from azure.identity import ManagedIdentityCredential
# from azure.keyvault.secrets import SecretClient
# credential = ManagedIdentityCredential()
# kv = SecretClient("https://myvault.vault.azure.net", credential)
# conn_str = kv.get_secret("sql-connection-string").value
Workload Identity Federation for AKS

For Kubernetes pods, individual pod-level Managed Identity requires Workload Identity Federation. The pod receives an OIDC token from the Kubernetes API server; Entra ID exchanges it for an Azure access token bound to a user-assigned MI.

# Enable OIDC issuer and workload identity on AKS cluster
az aks update     --name "aks-prod"     --resource-group "rg-production"     --enable-oidc-issuer     --enable-workload-identity

# Get the OIDC issuer URL
OIDC_ISSUER=$(az aks show     --name aks-prod     --resource-group rg-production     --query "oidcIssuerProfile.issuerUrl" -o tsv)

# Create a user-assigned MI for the workload
az identity create     --name "mi-aks-myapp"     --resource-group "rg-identity"

MI_CLIENT_ID=$(az identity show -n mi-aks-myapp -g rg-identity --query clientId -o tsv)
MI_PRINCIPAL=$(az identity show -n mi-aks-myapp -g rg-identity --query principalId -o tsv)

# Create the federated credential linking the K8s service account to the MI
az identity federated-credential create     --name "aks-myapp-federation"     --identity-name "mi-aks-myapp"     --resource-group "rg-identity"     --issuer "$OIDC_ISSUER"     --subject "system:serviceaccount:default:myapp-sa"     --audience "api://AzureADTokenExchange"

# Annotate the Kubernetes service account
kubectl annotate serviceaccount myapp-sa     azure.workload.identity/client-id="$MI_CLIENT_ID"     --namespace default
Troubleshooting Common Issues
Error
Cause
Fix
IMDS request fails with connection refused
MI not enabled on the resource
Enable system-assigned or assign user-assigned MI to the resource
401 Unauthorized when calling resource
MI's role assignment not yet propagated
Wait 2–5 min after role assignment; RBAC changes have propagation delay
Multiple MIs assigned — wrong one used
ManagedIdentityCredential() picks system-assigned first
Pass client_id=<mi-client-id> to target a specific user-assigned MI
MI can't access resource in another subscription
RBAC scope is wrong
Grant the role at the target subscription/resource scope, not the source subscription
AKS pod can't get token
Workload identity federated credential not configured or SA not annotated
Verify OIDC issuer, federated credential subject format, and SA annotation
Token Caching and Performance
The Azure SDK caches tokens in memory and refreshes them automatically before expiry. Understanding the cache behaviour helps you write efficient code and avoid unnecessary IMDS calls.
DefaultAzureCredential Chain

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential, ManagedIdentityCredential, ChainedTokenCredential
from azure.identity import AzureCliCredential, EnvironmentCredential

# DefaultAzureCredential tries these in order:
# 1. EnvironmentCredential (AZURE_CLIENT_ID, AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET, AZURE_TENANT_ID env vars)
# 2. WorkloadIdentityCredential (AKS workload identity)
# 3. ManagedIdentityCredential (IMDS endpoint)
# 4. SharedTokenCacheCredential (cached from developer tools)
# 5. VisualStudioCodeCredential
# 6. AzureCliCredential (az login session)
# 7. AzurePowerShellCredential
# 8. AzureDeveloperCliCredential (azd)

# In production (Azure resource with MI): hits #3 immediately
# In developer workstation: hits #6 (az login)
credential = DefaultAzureCredential()

# For explicit control in production — skip the chain, go straight to MI:
credential = ManagedIdentityCredential()  # fastest, no chain overhead

# For a user-assigned MI:
MI_CLIENT_ID = "xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx"
credential = ManagedIdentityCredential(client_id=MI_CLIENT_ID)

# The SDK caches tokens until 5 minutes before expiry (token lifetime is ~60 min)
# Each resource needs its own token — the cache is keyed by resource URI
Manual Token Acquisition for Non-SDK Calls

import requests, time

class MITokenCache:
    """Simple token cache for raw IMDS calls."""
    def __init__(self):
        self._tokens = {}  # resource -> {token, expires_at}

    def get_token(self, resource: str) -> str:
        entry = self._tokens.get(resource)
        if entry and entry["expires_at"] > time.time() + 300:  # 5 min buffer
            return entry["token"]

        resp = requests.get(
            "http://169.254.169.254/metadata/identity/oauth2/token",
            headers={"Metadata": "true"},
            params={"api-version": "2018-02-01", "resource": resource},
            timeout=10
        )
        resp.raise_for_status()
        data = resp.json()
        self._tokens[resource] = {
            "token": data["access_token"],
            "expires_at": int(data["expires_on"])
        }
        return data["access_token"]

cache = MITokenCache()

# Subsequent calls within the same process reuse the cached token
token1 = cache.get_token("https://management.azure.com/")
token2 = cache.get_token("https://vault.azure.net")   # separate token for KV
Managed Identity with Azure Key Vault: Full Pattern

The canonical use of a Managed Identity is to access Key Vault secrets at application startup — replacing connection strings in environment variables or config files. Here is the complete pattern including error handling.

import logging
from azure.identity import ManagedIdentityCredential, AzureCliCredential, ChainedTokenCredential
from azure.keyvault.secrets import SecretClient
from azure.core.exceptions import ResourceNotFoundError, ClientAuthenticationError

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

def get_app_config(vault_url: str) -> dict:
    """Load application secrets from Key Vault using MI (falls back to CLI for local dev)."""
    credential = ChainedTokenCredential(
        ManagedIdentityCredential(),   # used in Azure (production)
        AzureCliCredential()           # used locally (az login)
    )

    kv = SecretClient(vault_url=vault_url, credential=credential)
    secrets_to_load = ["sql-connection-string", "redis-password", "stripe-api-key"]

    config = {}
    for secret_name in secrets_to_load:
        try:
            config[secret_name] = kv.get_secret(secret_name).value
            logger.info("Loaded secret: %s", secret_name)
        except ResourceNotFoundError:
            logger.error("Secret not found: %s", secret_name)
            raise
        except ClientAuthenticationError as e:
            logger.error("MI authentication failed: %s", e)
            raise

    return config

# On startup:
app_config = get_app_config("https://myvault.vault.azure.net")
DB_CONN = app_config["sql-connection-string"]
Managed Identity with Azure SQL

Azure SQL Database supports Entra ID authentication. When your application connects using a Managed Identity, it acquires an Entra ID token and presents it instead of a username/password — no database credentials in your connection string.

import struct, pyodbc
from azure.identity import ManagedIdentityCredential

def get_sql_connection(server: str, database: str) -> pyodbc.Connection:
    credential = ManagedIdentityCredential()
    # Acquire token for Azure SQL
    token = credential.get_token("https://database.windows.net/.default")

    # pyodbc requires the token as raw bytes with a specific encoding
    token_bytes = token.token.encode("utf-16-le")
    token_struct = struct.pack(f"<I{len(token_bytes)}s", len(token_bytes), token_bytes)

    conn_str = (
        f"Driver={{ODBC Driver 18 for SQL Server}};"
        f"Server=tcp:{server},1433;"
        f"Database={database};"
        f"Encrypt=yes;TrustServerCertificate=no;"
        f"Connection Timeout=30;"
    )

    conn = pyodbc.connect(conn_str, attrs_before={1256: token_struct})
    return conn

# Grant the MI access in SQL Server:
# CREATE USER [mi-myapp] FROM EXTERNAL PROVIDER
# ALTER ROLE db_datareader ADD MEMBER [mi-myapp]
# ALTER ROLE db_datawriter ADD MEMBER [mi-myapp]
#
# Use the MI display name (not the client ID) in the SQL CREATE USER statement
Auditing Managed Identity Usage

Because Managed Identities authenticate silently, it is important to regularly audit which MIs exist, which resources they are assigned to, and what roles they hold — particularly for user-assigned MIs that can outlive the resources they were created for.

# Find all resources in a subscription that have a system-assigned MI
az resource list     --subscription "<sub-id>"     --query "[?identity.type=='SystemAssigned'].{Name:name, Type:type, MIPrincipal:identity.principalId}"     -o table

# List role assignments for all MIs (requires Reader at subscription scope)
az role assignment list     --subscription "<sub-id>"     --all     --query "[?principalType=='ServicePrincipal'].{Role:roleDefinitionName, Scope:scope, Principal:principalId}"     -o table | head -40
Managed Identity vs Other Credential Types: Decision Matrix
Scenario
Recommended Credential
VM / App Service / Functions accessing Key Vault, Storage, SQL
System-assigned Managed Identity
Fleet of VMs sharing the same database permissions
User-assigned Managed Identity (shared across all VMs)
AKS pod accessing Azure services
Workload Identity (federated credential on user-assigned MI)
GitHub Actions deploying to Azure
Federated credential on service principal (no secrets)
Azure DevOps pipeline deploying to Azure
Workload identity federation or MI on self-hosted agent
On-premises server accessing Azure API
Service principal with certificate credential
Developer machine (local testing)
AzureCliCredential (az login) or DefaultAzureCredential
Third-party SaaS needing Azure access
Service principal with certificate; rotate annually
Summary

Managed Identities are the preferred authentication mechanism for any workload running on Azure. The platform provisions and rotates all credentials automatically; your code acquires tokens by calling the IMDS endpoint at 169.254.169.254 — no secrets to store, rotate, or accidentally expose.
System-assigned: tied to one resource, deleted when the resource is deleted — ideal for unique per-resource identity
User-assigned: independent lifecycle, shareable across many resources — ideal for fleets and shared configurations
The Azure SDK's ManagedIdentityCredential handles IMDS automatically; pass client_id= to target a user-assigned MI
For Kubernetes pod-level identity, use AKS Workload Identity with federated credentials
Grant RBAC roles to the MI's principal ID (not the resource); allow 2–5 min for propagation
Use Managed Identity everywhere possible — fall back to federated credentials for external CI/CD; use service principal secrets only as a last resort
